title_content.content: Amplitude-modulated resonant accelerometer employing parametric pump

title_content.content: Amplitude-modulated resonant accelerometer employing parametric pump

item_content.content: Department of Engineering, The Nanoscience Centre, University of Cambridge, Cambridge CB3 0FF, United Kingdom
Silicon Microgravity Ltd., Cambridge, Innovation Park, Cambridge CB25 9PB, United Kingdom

MOE Key Laboratory of Fundamental Physical Quantities Measurement and Hubei Key Laboratory of Gravitation and Quantum Physics, PGMF and School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China
Author to whom correspondence should be addressed: hz358@cam.ac.uk

paragraph_content.content: This paper demonstrates a resonant accelerometer employing weakly coupled resonators for inertial force transduction. An ultra-low virtual mode coupling is established by employing the parametric pump in the coupled resonators to enhance the parametric sensitivity. This paper proposes that the amplitude of the driven resonator can be used for monitoring the input acceleration in a limited linear range around the veering point. In addition to the high-sensitivity improvement, the measurement complexity is also considerably reduced compared to the previous amplitude ratio readout sensors. The sensitivity is adjustable by changing the parametric pumping amplitude. The experimental results show that the noise power spectral density is
which is the best reported noise floor for resonant accelerometers based on weakly coupled resonators.
Published under license by AIP Publishing. https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0019296
Micromechanical resonators have attracted significant attention owing to their promise for extensive applications in areas related to timing and frequency control
Resonant accelerometers, which transduce the input acceleration to frequency variations, have been extensively studied because of their potential applications in navigation and seismometry.
Resonant transducers based on the vibration mode localization

principle have emerged in recent years due to the demonstrated improvements in parametric sensitivities and passive common-mode rejection. In mode-localized sensors, the ratio of the motional amplitudes of the coupled resonators instead of the conventional frequency has been employed as the output metric.
applying this principle to design accelerometers have demonstrated a noise floor of
, benchmarking favorably with respect to other low-g commercial MEMS accelerometers.

The classical mode-localized accelerometer consists of a structurally symmetric coupled resonator system, a suspended proof mass, and a lever system for inertial force amplification, as shown in Fig. 1(a). Key parameters of the device can be found in Fig. 1(b). One end of the driven resonator (Res 1) is connected to a proof mass via the lever system, while the other end is coupled via a beam with a secondary resonator (Res 2) whose opposite end is fixed; thus, a fixed

mechanical coupling between the resonators is achieved, as shown schematically in Fig. 1(c). Acceleration applied to the proof mass will induce a tensile or compressive axial strain in the resonating beam of Res 1, resulting in stiffness variations and breaking the symmetry of the coupled resonators. Vibration energy confinement

then occurs and the ratio of the motional amplitudes (or amplitude ratio, AR) changes so that the applied acceleration can be tracked by monitoring the AR variation. The AR scale factor is defined by the coupling factor

the stiffness ratio between the coupling beam and the resonating beam, which can be determined by the frequency splitting between the vibration modes. A lower coupling factor results in a higher scale factor
and, thus, a higher input-referred noise floor, if electronics noise dominates the resolution; therefore, most of the current attempts have been directed toward decreasing the coupling factor.
However, an ultra-weak mechanical coupling using the traditional flexural coupling beam is difficult to implement from a practical perspective.
We have recently reported that a parametric pump
can further reduce the coupling and improve the AR sensitivity by a factor of 183.
Yet, the complexity of the AR readout increases as precisely demodulating the dynamic Stokes-sideband can be difficult especially when it is positioned several Hz away from its neighboring mode.

paragraph_content.content: In this paper, instead of using the AR, we only measure the amplitude of the driven resonator (Res 1) to monitor the external acceleration. In addition to demonstrating the high sensitivity and resolution, this method also considerably decreases the detection complexity in comparison with AR-based accelerometers[19,25] as only the amplitude of a single resonator in a specific mode must now be tracked.

The coupled resonators can be modeled as a mass-spring-damper model system as shown in Fig. 2(a). The effective stiffness of Res 1 is modified by the stiffness perturbations resulting from the external acceleration
. The effective stiffness of Res 2 varies over time due to the additional periodic electrostatic force generated by the pump signal and

is experimentally examined with a view toward determining key accelerometer metrics.

To obtain the detection limit of the accelerometer, closed-loop measurements are mandatory and the effective bandwidth should be carefully studied. In the closed-loop measurement, PLL was used to lock Res 1 in a certain mode by adjusting the phase setpoint

until it reaches the same value as that of the resonant peak in the open-loop sweep. We then used the electrostatic force for applying body loads to the proof mass to mimic dynamic acceleration (see supplementary material Note 2). Based on the calibration, it is found that a voltage of
pp applied to the comb drives corresponds to an acceleration of
(see Fig. S2). Figure 5(c) shows the responses of the closed-loop amplitudes of Res 1 vs a sinusoidal acceleration input with a peak of
. The sensitivity curve is obtained and shown in Fig. 5(d) by tuning
per step in the closed-loop setup and the fitted sensitivity is

However, the scale factor is not constant but will change as the frequency of the input acceleration increases, which leads to bandwidth limitations. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the maximum amplitude change (between the peak and the valley) will decrease, i.e., the sensitivity decreases, as the acceleration frequency increases. A fit to the response of the sensitivity vs acceleration frequency can be seen in Fig. 5(f). An estimated bandwidth
sensitivity drop as the cutoff frequency.

A recording of the sensor noise floor was conducted in the University Laboratory Environment, where ambient vibrations are inevitable. The input-referred noise power spectral density (NPSD) from a 2-h representative single recording is shown in Fig. 6(a). When an electrostatically mimicked sinusoidal acceleration with a frequency of
is applied, we can see the peaks at
and the sidebands from Fig. 6(a) (red curve). The curve shows clearly a peak around
, resulting from the ambient vibration and a peak around
, indicating the coupling mode [Fig. 2(c)]. The input-referred noise floor of the accelerometer is
with an integration time of 1 s as shown in Fig. 6(b). The white noise floor[27] of the sensor can also be evaluated from the velocity random walk (VRW)
region of the Allan deviation plot, demonstrating good consistency with the NPSD calculation.
In summary, this paper reports a high-resolution amplitude-modulated resonant accelerometer based on weakly coupled resonators employing a parametric pump. A noise floor of

is obtained, representing a threefold improvement compared to the previously reported weakly coupled resonant sensors employing a parametric pump[25] and one order of magnitude over the state-of-the-art mode-localized accelerometers,[19] using the same device. However, the linear measurement range is limitedly attributed to the inevitable trade-off with sensitivity in such devices, while the scale factor is adjustable by changing the pumping amplitude. The resolution of the current device is still limited by the electronic noise; thus, the device enables the measurement over a dynamic range less than
, which can be further improved to bring this specification into better alignment with other sub-

accelerometers.[28] With further optimization in the input-referred acceleration resolution/instability by decreasing the electronic noise and incorporating temperature control (to suppress fluctuations), the proposed accelerometer has the potential to be applied to seismic measurements,[29] where the measurement range and bandwidth constraints are acceptable. Furthermore, this sensitive transduction technique can also be extended to other resonant sensing application domains with similar requirements.
See the supplementary material for derivations of the mode coupling and the mimicked acceleration.
